From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [32 Jazz](/w/index.php?title=32_Jazz&redirect=no "32 Jazz"))

Record label

| 32 Records | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Founded | 1995 (1995) |
| Founder | [Joel Dorn](/wiki/Joel_Dorn "Joel Dorn"), Robert Miller |
| Defunct | 2001 (2001) |
| Genre | [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") |
| Country of origin | U.S. |
| Location | New York City |

**32 Records** was a [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") established in 1995 by record producer [Joel Dorn](/wiki/Joel_Dorn "Joel Dorn") and attorney Robert Miller.[[1]](#cite_note-jb1997-1)[[2]](#cite_note-bailey-2)[[3]](#cite_note-hunter-3) Its **32 Jazz** [imprint](/wiki/Imprint_(trade_name) "Imprint (trade name)") released a successful series of [compilation albums](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album").[[2]](#cite_note-bailey-2)[[3]](#cite_note-hunter-3)[[4]](#cite_note-sg1998-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sg1999-5)[[6]](#cite_note-gm1999-6) It was named for Dorn's favorite [sports number](/wiki/Number_(sports) "Number (sports)").[[1]](#cite_note-jb1997-1) It also released new material by artists such as [The Jazz Passengers](/wiki/The_Jazz_Passengers "The Jazz Passengers") and established 32 R&B, 32 Blues, 32 Groove, and 32 Pop subsidiaries.[[1]](#cite_note-jb1997-1)[[2]](#cite_note-bailey-2)

32 Records acquired the holdings of the [Muse](/wiki/Muse_Records "Muse Records") and [Landmark](/wiki/Landmark_Records "Landmark Records") labels, and established the 32 Jazz subsidiary in 1997 with the aim of re-issuing numerous [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") recordings.[[1]](#cite_note-jb1997-1)[[2]](#cite_note-bailey-2)[[7]](#cite_note-gitler-7)[[8]](#cite_note-jacobs-8) During the late 1990s, 32 Jazz released a successful series of inexpensive "Jazz for..." compilations.[[4]](#cite_note-sg1998-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sg1999-5) The first of these, *Jazz for a Rainy Afternoon*, was released in 1997 in conjunction with [*Elle*](/wiki/Elle_(magazine) "Elle (magazine)").[[4]](#cite_note-sg1998-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sg1999-5) In 1999, it had become the top jazz label in the [*Billboard* charts](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts"), before Verve Music Group merged its [Verve](/wiki/Verve_Records "Verve Records") and [GRP](/wiki/GRP_Records "GRP Records") holdings into a single output.[[6]](#cite_note-gm1999-6)

By 2000, Miller had become [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of CDBeat, which owned 32 Records as a subsidiary.[[9]](#cite_note-32pr2000-9) In March 2000, Dorn resigned from the company and formed Label M later that year.[[10]](#cite_note-il1999-10)[[11]](#cite_note-waller-11) Upon Dorn's departure, CDBeat announced that it would discontinue 32 Records by March 2001,[[12]](#cite_note-cdbeat-12) although in June 2000 it hired producer [Todd Barkan](/wiki/Todd_Barkan "Todd Barkan") to replace Dorn.[[9]](#cite_note-32pr2000-9)[[11]](#cite_note-waller-11)[[13]](#cite_note-margolis-13) CDBeat, in turn, would become Spinrocket and then ConnectivCorp.[[14]](#cite_note-spinrocket2000-14) [Savoy Jazz](/wiki/Savoy_Records "Savoy Records") acquired the rights to the Muse and Landmark catalogs from 32 Records in October 2003,[[15]](#cite_note-porter-15) and by the end of the year, ConnectivCorp had merged with [Majesco Entertainment](/wiki/Majesco_Entertainment "Majesco Entertainment").[[16]](#cite_note-connectivcorp-16)
